coredata.dc:title: Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS quantum dot nanoprobe with photoluminescence emission enhancement for labeling cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Naiqiang
Liu, Ling
Zhao, Shihua
coredata.dc:description: Ag nanobox/silica/quantum dot (QD) nanoprobe was synthesized by using a hydrothermal method to investigate the interaction between Ag nanobox and CdTeS QDs for the first time. Finite-difference time domain method was used to compare the optical behavior of Ag nanobox with Ag nanosquare. The comparison demonstrated that the Ag nanobox had excellent optical characteristics. In the nanoprobe, the silica shell was used to adjust the distance between the Ag nanobox and CdTeS QDs. A 4.5-fold increase in the fluorescence intensity of CdTeS QDs was achieved based on the local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of Ag nanobox. The Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe was used to label cancer cells, thereby indicating that the nanoprobe was a suitable and excellent fluorescence probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coating materials
Optical materials
Chemical synthesis
Computer simulations

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Ag nanobox/CdTeS QD nanoprobe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11647019 11704237 , 51501128 and U1704145 ), the Henan Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 172102210435 ), and the Henan Provincial Educational Committee Program (No. 17B430004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The e-field distribution of Ag nanosphere (radius: 30 nm) at the x-z plane with y = 0 (a), Ag nanobox (side length: 60 nm) at the x-z plane with y = 0 (b) and y = 3 × 10−8 m (c).
The preparation progress of the Ag nanobox/CdTeS QDs nanoparticle.
The TEM images of obtained Ag nanobox (a), the absorption spectrum of Ag nanobox with experiment (b) and absorption spectrum of Ag nanobox with simulation (c).
The TEM images of Ag nanobox/silica with different thickness of the silica (5 nm (a), 10 nm (b), 25 nm (c)), and the TEM images of Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QDs.
A series of absorption spectra of Ag nanobox/silica with different thickness of the silica, which was adjusted by the volume of TEOS.
The fluorescence spectra of the bare CdTeS QDs and Ag nanobox/CdTeS QDs with different thickness of silica, which was controlled by the volume of TEOS.
A brief illustration of the enhancement progress (a) and quenching progress (b) of CdTeS QDs.
Cancer viability investigation of MDA-MB-231 cancer cells incubated with Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QDs nanoprobe.
The images of cancer cells labeling with bare CdTeS QDs (a) and Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QDs nanoprobe (b, the nuclei were stained in the inset image).

head.ce:title: Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS quantum dot nanoprobe with photoluminescence emission enhancement for labeling cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical & Electronic Engineering, Shangqiu Normal University, Shangqiu, 476000, Henan, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Tianjin Polytechnic University, 300387, Tianjin, People's Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physics and Electrical Information, Shangqiu Normal University, Shangqiu, 476000, Henan, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electrical Information

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ag nanobox/silica/quantum dot (QD) nanoprobe was synthesized by using a hydrothermal method to investigate the interaction between Ag nanobox and CdTeS QDs for the first time. Finite-difference time domain method was used to compare the optical behavior of Ag nanobox with Ag nanosquare. The comparison demonstrated that the Ag nanobox had excellent optical characteristics. In the nanoprobe, the silica shell was used to adjust the distance between the Ag nanobox and CdTeS QDs. A 4.5-fold increase in the fluorescence intensity of CdTeS QDs was achieved based on the local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of Ag nanobox. The Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe was used to label cancer cells, thereby indicating that the nanoprobe was a suitable and excellent fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, the development of nanotechnology has greatly promoted the progress of technological innovation [1–5]. For example, metallic nanoparticles have received much attention due to their LSPR [6,7], a collective resonance phenomenon of free conduction-band electrons, that could boost the absorption coefficient and quantum yield of adjacent chromophores. Metallic nanoparticles are widely researched in various fields, such as photothermal therapy [8,9], fluorescent labeling [10–12] based on the LSPR. Xia et al. reported the gold nanocage with intense absorption coefficient as a photothermal nanoprobe [13] to realize its function of destroying targeted cancer cells. Li et al. studied metal-enhanced fluorescence in gold core/porous silica shell encapsulated with Y2O3:Eu [14]. Recently, our group obtained the structure of Ag core/silica shell implanted with dye molecule, realizing an enhancement factor up to three times higher than the bare dye molecule [15]. These results show that metallic nanoparticles have an excellent LSPR effect.

Fluorescent technology has become an important detection tool in medicine, biology and electronics [16,17]. In the area of biomedicine, organic dye was used as a traditional fluorescent probe due to its high fluorescent intensity and narrow spectra. However, the fluorescent lifetimes of organic dye were relatively lower, thereby limiting its widespread application in various fields [18]. In recent years, QDs, as a new generation of fluorescent probes, received increasing attention by science researchers. The advantages of QDs over organic dye were their large Stokes shift, long lifetime and good stability [19,20]. However, the fluorescent intensity of QDs was limited, thus seriously restricting their further applications. A range of metallic sphere core/QDs-doped silica shell nanoprobes were synthesized [21,22] to improve fluorescent intensity. The fluorescent intensity of QDs was enhanced based on the LSPR effect of metallic sphere. For example, Li et al. reported that a more than fourfold increase in the fluorescence intensity of QDs was obtained in their Ag sphere core/QDs-doped silica nanoprobe [14].

Recently, several studies verified that the LSPR effect of metallic materials was related to the size, shape and composition of the materials. Liu et al. studied the enhancement effect of Ag nanoparticle on CdSe QDs and found that worm-like Ag nanoparticles had a higher enhancement effect than the Ag sphere [23]. Here, a designed an Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe was designed and the LSPR effect of Ag nanobox on the CdTeS QDs was investigated. With the use of theoretical calculation, the advantage of Ag nanobox was emphasized through the finite difference time domain (FDTD) method. The thickness of the silica shell was adjusted to the distance of metal-QDs to obtain the largest fluorescence enhancement effect of the Ag nanobox. Finally, the Ag nanobox/QDs nanoprobe was used to target cancer cells.

Deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm−1), absolute ethanol (99.7%, AR), silver trifluoroacetate (CF3COOAg, AR), ethylene glycol (EG, AR), hydrochloric acid (36% HCl), sodium hydrosulfide (NaHS, AR), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 55000), cadmium chloride (99.0%), tellurium powder (99.5%), 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 99+%, Sigma-Aldrich), ascorbic acid (99.7%, AR), ammonia (25%–28% NH3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 99%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (28.4% SiO2), 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APS, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich), phosphate buffered saline (PBS, AR), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), high glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM), breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231, Anhui Medical University). The other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All cell culture media and supplements were obtained from Anhui Medical University. CdTeS QDs were synthesized by our group.

The preparation progress of the Ag nanobox was consistent with that in our previous report with minor modification [24]. Liquid-phase reduction method was used to synthesize the Ag nanobox with CF3COOAg and EG as the precursor and the reducing agent, respectively. After refluxing 10 mL EG solution was refluxed at 140 °C for 20 min in a magnetron oil bath pan, 200 μL of 2 mM NaHS solution was dropped into the reaction solution through rapid magnetic stirring. As the mixing was completed, 2.5 mL of 0.167 M PVP solution was titrated into the mixture, followed by the addition of 0.8 mL of 0.3 M CF3COOAg solution. Subsequently, the mixture was refluxed at 130 °C under vigorous stirring. The size and shape of the Ag nanobox can be controlled by the reaction time. Finally, the Ag nanobox was collected from the reaction solution through multiple centrifugations and dispersed in 10 mL deionized water for next usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ag nanobox/CdTeS QD nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: First, a modified Stöber method was used to prepare the core-shell Ag nanobox/SiO2 shell composite [25]. Then, 1 mL Ag nanobox solution was mixed with 10 mL absolute ethanol under vigorous stirring, followed by the addition of 0.5 mL ammonium hydroxide. As the mixture became homogenous solution, TEOS solution was added to the reaction mixture under low-speed stirring and the reaction lasted for 12 h at room temperature. The dosage of TEOS solution can be used to tune the thinness of the SiO2 shell, which can control the distance between Ag box and QDs. Subsequently, the Ag nanobox/SiO2 nanoparticle was washed with a large amount of ethanol solution and was re-dispersed in 10 mL ethanol solution. Second, APS (a silane coupling agent with -NH2 groups) was used to function onto the surface of Ag nanobox/SiO2 nanoparticle, which was suitable for the interlinkage between the nanobox/SiO2 nanoparticle and QDs. A total of 50 μL APS was pipetted into the Ag nanobox/SiO2 solution under moderate stirring. As the mixture reacted for 6 h at room temperature, the mixture was washed and re-dispersed in 10 mL ethanol solution. Third, highly fluorescent CdTeS QDs were linked onto the Ag nanobox/SiO2 nanoparticle on the basis of the strong bonds between carboxyl and -NH2 groups. A total of 50 μL of carboxyl-modified CdTeS QDs and 5 mL Ag nanobox/SiO2 nanoparticle were mixed under vigorous stirring for 6 h. Finally, 1 mL of folic acid was used to function onto the surface of Ag nanobox/SiO2 to improve the uptake capability of cancer cell for Ag nanobox/CdTeS QD nanoprobe. Similar to this progress, bare CdTeS QDs was also been functioned with folic acid.

The cytotoxicity of the Ag nanobox/CdTeS QD nanoprobe on the breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) was evaluated by MTT assay. The nanoprobes with different concentrations ranging from 0 to 18 mg/mL were prepared. As cancer cells were treated by the nanoprobes for 24 h, MTT method was used to assess the cell viability, which was expressed in the form of percentage of surviving cells. Six wells of cancer cells were set as the zero group only with fresh DMEM, while another 12 wells were set as the control group with any addition of different concentrations of the nanoprobes. The cell viability was calculated using A control/A zero × 100%. All the dates were measured for five times and calculate the mean values.

MDA-MB-231 cancer cells were seeded on a glass coverslip in a 12-well culture plate and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2 (v/v). As the coverage of cancer cells on the glass coverslip reached approximately 60%–80%, the coverslip was washed with a large amount of PBS solution. Subsequently, 50 μL Ag nanobox/QD nanoprobes were titrated onto the glass coverslip and incubated for another 1 h at 37 °C. Finally, the cancer cells were washed again with PBS solution and allowed to dry overnight.

Microstructure of the nanoparticle and nanoprobe was recorded with a JEM-1400 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL Co., Ltd., Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 120 kV. The UV–visible absorption spectra and emission spectra were obtained with a Shimadzu UV-2401 spectrophotometer and Shimadzu RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The image of cancer cells were obtained by Olympus Bx53 Mons fluorescence microscope. The absorption spectrum and electromagnetic field distribution of nanoparticle were simulated by the three-dimensional FDTD solution method.

Through the FDTD solution method, Ag nanosphere model (radius: 30 nm) and Ag nanobox model (side length: 60 nm) were computed, whose centers were set on the origin of coordinates of the simulation system. The direction of the incident light was along the Y-axis, whereas that of the polarization was along the X-axis. Fig. 1 shows the electric field (e-field) distribution of the Ag nanosphere and nanobox with their corresponding best excitation wavelength. Fig. 1a (x-z plane with y = 0) shows an intensive e-field around the Ag nanosphere, which was consistent with previous report. The x–z plane with y = 0 and y = 3 × 10−8 m are shown in Fig. 1b and c to display the e-field distribution of the Ag nanobox, respectively. Compared with the Ag nanosphere, the Ag nanobox generated a larger enhancement factor, which reached up to four orders of magnitude, especially in the corner and edge. The Ag nanobox was used to enhance the fluorescence intensity of QDs in the Ag nanobox/CdTeS QD nanoparticle based on the principle.

Fig. 2 shows the preparation progress of the Ag nanobox/CdTeS QD nanoparticle. The Ag nanobox was synthesized through oxidation–reduction reaction using CF3COOAg as the precursor of silver ion. When uniform Ag nanobox was obtained, the surface of the Ag nanobox was coated with silica and modified with APS. Subsequently, CdTeS QDs stabilized with the carboxyl group were linked onto the surface of the Ag nanobox/silica on the basis of the strong chemical bond between carboxyl and amino groups. Lastly, Ag nanobox/CdTeS QD nanoparticle was decorated with folic acid, which can improve the biocompatibility and stability of the nanoprobe.

Silver nanobox was obtained by reducing silver ion with EG and PVP as reducing agent and stabilizer., respectively. The main mechanism was as follows:
(1) 2HOCH2CH2OH → 2CH3CHO + 2H2O
(2) 2Ag+ + 2CH3CHO → CH3CO-OCCH3 + 2Ag + 2H+

Fig. 3 a shows the TEM image of the obtained Ag nanobox with good dispersity and uniformity. Such nanobox was obtained with the reaction time of 60 min. According to the statistics of particle size, the size of the Ag nanobox was 60 ± 3 nm. There are several approximate sphere Ag nanoparticles among the nanoboxes. The reason was that the test sample was dispread in ammonium hydroxide before the TEM characterizations. In this progress, the Ag nanobox had certain corrosion due to the alkalinity of ammonium hydroxide, so the edges and corners of several Ag nanobox were smoothed. Fig. 3b (red line) shows that the absorption spectrum of the Ag nanobox exhibited three distinct peaks. The long wavelength peak corresponded to the dipole mode. The middle peak was attributed to the quadrupole resonance mode. The short wavelength peak, including quadrupole and dipole components, was derived from the hybrid mode. All the three absorption peaks had a narrow full width at half maximum, which was consistent with the uniform particle size distribution of the Ag nanobox. The absorption peak of the Ag nanobox (side length: 60 nm) was simulated using FDTD solution method, as shown in Fig. 3b (black line). The simulation results were consistent with our experimental results.

Previous reports on metal-enhanced fluorescence indicate that the fluorescent material should not be in direct contact with the surface of the metal nanoparticle [26,27]; otherwise, fluorescence quenching will occur. Silica was typically used as an isolation layer between metal nanoparticle and fluorescent material due to its excellent optical transparency and biocompatibility. The thickness of the isolation layer mainly affects metal-enhanced fluorescence. Fig. 4 a–c show the TEM images of the Ag nanobox/silica with different silica thickness. In the experiment, the thickness of the silica was positively correlated with the volume of TEOS. However, Fig. 4 shows that the deformation of the Ag nanobox was poorer than the volume of ammonium hydroxide increment due to the strong causticity of ammonium hydroxide. Fig. 5 shows a series of absorption spectra of the Ag nanobox/silica with different silica thickness. The dipole resonant absorption peak of the Ag nanobox/silica nanoparticles gradually shifted toward the long wavelength with an increase in the thickness of the silica because the dipole absorption peak of the nanoparticle was positively correlated with its surrounding dielectric constant. In the structure of the Ag nanobox/silica, the silica had a larger dielectric constant (εshell = 2.12) than the solvent (H2O, εH2O = 1.78) or ambient air [28]. Fig. 4d shows the TEM image of the Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QDs (silica thickness of 10 nm). The CdTeS QDs were successfully linked onto the surface of the Ag nanobox/silica. Moreover, the Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoparticle was uniform and disperses evenly in the solvent.

Fig. 6 shows the temperature fluorescence spectra of the bare CdTeS QDs and Ag nanobox/CdTeS QDs with different silica thickness, excited by a wavelength of 420 nm. When suitable TEOS was added to the reaction solution, the fluorescence intensity of CdTeS QDs was enhanced by the LSPR effect of the Ag nanobox due to the overlap of the excitation wavelength with the LSPR wavelength of the Ag nanobox. The resonant excitation of LSPR on the surface of the Ag nanobox produced strongly enhanced local fields, thereby considerably increasing the fluorescence intensity of CdTeS QDs. This phenomenon was reported in previous studies [29]. The LSPR effect of metallic nanoparticle can improve the fluorescence of CdTeS QDs. Although every sample had the same concentrations of QD, their fluorescence spectra showed different intensities. When 1 μL of TEOS participated in the Stöber reaction, the silica thickness of Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoparticle was approximately 10 nm and the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum value. When the silica thickness was thinner than 10 nm, fluorescence quenching occurred due to the short distance between the Ag nanobox and the CdTeS QDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QDs with thicker silica was weakened compared with the bare CdTeS QDs because the large distance between Ag nanobox and CdTeS QDs reduced the LSPR effect of Ag nanobox. Fig. 7 provides a brief illustration of the enhancement and quenching progress of CdTeS QDs, which was in the vicinity of the Ag nanobox, to clearly illustrate the mechanism of metal-enhanced fluorescence. The fluorescence enhancement of the CdTeS QDs was the consequence of more electron–hole pairs generated by the Ag nanobox under resonance excitation, whereas fluorescence quenching was the result of electron transfer from CdTeS QDs to the Ag nanobox. The best method to prevent quenching was to establish a certain distance between CdTeS QDs and the Ag nanobox.

As an excellent nanoprobes, the cytotoxicity is a significant factor for their potential application. A standard MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the prepared Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe, which was shown in Fig. 8 . The relative cell viability of Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe was 91.3% even at a concentration of up to 41 mg/mL. These results indicated that the Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe with good biocompatibility was suitable for biological research. Cell labeling applications were further explored based on the high fluorescence of the Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe. Cancer cells have strong phagocytosis to folic acid. Folic acid was modified onto the surface of Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QDs. Fig. 9 a and b shows the images of cancer cells that are labeled with bare CdTeS QDs and Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe, respectively. To distinguish cytoplasm from organelles, the nuclei were stained with DAPI, which was shown in the inset of Fig. 9b. The brightness and definition of the images using the Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QDs were better than those of the bare CdTeS QDs.

In summary, a novel Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe was proposed and synthesized for labeling cancer cells for the first time. The structure of the Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QDs shows that the fluorescence intensity was tuned by controlling the thickness of the silica. A more than 4.5-fold increase in the fluorescence intensity of CdTeS QDs was obtained at the silica shell thickness of 10 nm. The Ag nanobox/silica/CdTeS QD nanoprobe realized the labeling of cancer cells, and its labeling image was superior to that of bare CdTeS QDs. This work is an important step toward the use of the LSPR effect of the Ag nanobox to enhance the fluorescence of CdTeS QDs. Taking advantage of metal-enhanced fluorescence is beneficial for higher-quality labeling applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11647019 11704237, 51501128 and U1704145), the Henan Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 172102210435), and the Henan Provincial Educational Committee Program (No. 17B430004).
